title: A Tragedy Of The 19th Century.
content:
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻"Et potis es nigrum vitio praefigere Delta." - PERSIUS.


혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻It was a young Examiner, scarce thirty were his years,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻His name our University loves, honours, and reveres:
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻He pondered o'er some papers, and a tear stood in his eye;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻He split his quill upon the desk, and raised a bitter cry - 
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'O why has Fortune struck me down with this unearthly blow?
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻"Why doom'd me to examine in my lov'd one's Little-go?
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻"O Love and Duty, sisters twain, in diverse ways ye pull;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻"I dare not 'pass,' I scarce can 'pluck:' my cup of woe is full.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻"O that I ever should have lived this dismal day to see"!
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻He knit his brow, and nerved his hand, and wrote the fatal D.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻*혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻*혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻*혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻*혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻*혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻*

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻It was a lovely maiden down in Hertford's lovely shire;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Before her on a reading-desk, lay many a well-filled quire:
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻The lamp of genius lit her eyes; her years were twenty-two;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Her brow was high, her cheek was pale, her bearing somewhat blue:
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻She pondered o'er a folio, and laboured to divine
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻The mysteries of "x" and "y," and many a magic sign:
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Yet now and then she raised her eye, and ceased awhile to ponder,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And seem'd as though inclined to allow her thoughts elsewhere to wander,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻A step was heard, she closed her book; her heart beat high and fast,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻As through the court and up the stairs a manly figure passed.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻One moment more, the opening door disclosed unto her view
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Her own beloved Examiner, her friend and lover true.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻"Tell me, my own Rixator, is it First or Second Class?"
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻His firm frame shook, he scarce could speak, he only sigh'd "Alas!"
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻She gazed upon him with an air serenely calm and proud - 
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻"Nay, tell me all, I fear it not" - he murmured sadly "Ploughed."
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻She clasped her hands, she closed her eyes as fell the word of doom;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Full five times round in silence did she pace her little room;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Then calmly sat before her books, and sigh'd "Rixator dear,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻"Give me the list of subjects to be studied for next year."

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻"My own brave Mathematica, my pupil and my pride,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻"My persevering Student whom I destine for my bride;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻"Love struggled hard with Duty, while the lover marked you B;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻"In the end the stern Examiner prevailed and gave you D.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻"Mine was the hand that dealt the blow!혻혻혻혻Alas, against my will
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻"I plucked you in Arithmetic - and can'st thou love me still?"
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻She gazed upon him and her eye was full of love and pride - 
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻"Nay these are but the trials, Love, by which true love is tried.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻"I never knew your value true, until you marked me D:
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻"D stands for dear, and dear to me you evermore shall be."

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻*혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻*혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻*혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻*혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻*혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻*

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻A year had passed, and she had passed, for morning, noon, and night,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Her Euclid and her Barnard-Smith had been her sole delight.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Soon "Baccalaurea Artium" was added to her name,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And Hitchin's groves, and Granta's courts resounded with her fame;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And when Rixator hurried down one day by the express,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And asked if she would have him, I believe she answered "Yes."
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻For now they live together, and a wiser, happier pair,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻More learned and more loving, can scarce be found elsewhere;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And they teach their children Euclid, and their babies all can speak
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻French and German in their cradles, and at five can write good Greek;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And he is a Professor and she Professoress,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And they never cease the Little-go in gratitude to bless;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻When love could not the Lover from the path of duty sway,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And no amount of plucking could his Student fair dismay.


혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻MORAL.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Faint heart ne'er won fair lady, if in love you would have luck,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻In wooing, as in warfare, trust in nothing else than pluck.

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻(1871).
meaning:
Edward Woodley Bowling's contribution to classic is further solidified by the brilliance found in "A Tragedy Of The 19th Century."...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
